The structural argument is sovereignty: open weights let European states and companies inspect, customize, and run models on their own infrastructure, while closed US providers can have access revoked by Washington overnight. Mistral is still the small player, with €3.5bn raised and a reported €20bn round in talks against Anthropic's $965bn, but access risk is now its sharpest selling point.

title: This French startup’s AI is already calling shots on battlefields. Now, it’s raised €32M to scale
blurb: Comand AI, a Paris startup whose AI command-and-control platform Prevail is already running with operational units in Ukraine, France, and Germany, raised €32M in a Series A led by Blossom Capital. Saab joined as a strategic backer, a sign that Europe's established defense industry would rather invest in AI-native command software than build it in-house. The bet is the decision layer that sits above sensors, drones, and weapons, a gap Helsing at $18B and Anduril at $61B left open, and the open question is whether a €32M startup can claim it before the giants move down into it.

title: French battlefield software startup Comand AI raises €32m from Blossom Capital, Saab
blurb: Comand AI raised €32m in Series A funding led by Blossom Capital, with Swedish defence prime Saab and Polish investor Expeditions joining. The Paris startup builds an AI platform that helps military officers plan, run, and analyze operations, with a stated goal of conducting them four times faster with four times fewer people. The platform is already deployed with units in France, Germany, and Ukraine, and the round funds expansion across NATO markets and into air and maritime domains, as European defence software draws sharper investor attention.

title: CuspAI to hit $2.6bn valuation with backing from Jeff Bezos, reports say
blurb: CuspAI is raising $400m at a $2.6bn valuation, with Jeff Bezos's family office and Kleiner Perkins joining a round that more than quadruples the $100m mark it set last September. The Cambridge startup runs what amounts to a search engine for materials, with one set of AI models proposing new molecules for uses like sustainable energy and AI compute and another set predicting how they'd perform in the real world. Investors are paying up for AI applied to a hard science problem, not another software wrapper, and a board with Yann LeCun and Geoffrey Hinton is part of what they're buying.

title: After taking Nvidia’s money, Odyssey raises $310M and bets on Amazon and AMD instead
blurb: Odyssey raised $310M at a $1.45B valuation to build general-purpose world models, with Amazon and AMD Ventures joining a round that names AWS as preferred cloud and hands the lab Amazon's Trainium chips. The pointed part is the timing, since Nvidia's venture arm backed Odyssey's Series A just four months ago, and the startup is now wiring its future compute to Nvidia's challengers instead. With 55 employees and only $27M raised before this, Odyssey is one of the most capital-intensive AI bets per head on the market.

title: Deutsches Roboter-Startup sammelt 1,4 Milliarden Dollar ein
blurb: Neura Robotics, a German humanoid-robot startup, raised up to $1.4B in a Series C from Amazon, Nvidia, Qualcomm, Bosch, Schaeffler, Tether, and the European Investment Bank, which it says is the largest round ever for a full-stack robotics company. The money goes into manufacturing and its "Neuraverse" platform, where robots are meant to share learned skills, with a stated goal of producing several million units by 2030 against a current order book above $1B. It positions Europe as a credible challenger to the US and Chinese players racing to own physical AI.

title: Sixty percent of US consumers say 'AI' in brand messaging is a turnoff
blurb: Brands have spent two years and real budget chasing AI brand visibility, yet 61% of US consumers can't name a single company doing it well and 60% say "AI" in brand messaging is a turnoff rather than a feature. The deeper problem is trust: 74% say the web feels less human than a decade ago, and bot fatigue sets in after about 40 minutes. The category has no incumbent and no template, so the first brand to feed AI engines clean data while still giving humans a reason to stay gets to define the standard.

title: PayPal Ventures shutters as company restructuring continues
blurb: PayPal is winding down PayPal Ventures, its corporate venture arm since 2016, pausing new investments and hiring Jefferies to explore secondary sales of holdings that include Plaid, Anchorage Digital, and Talos Global. The move is part of CEO Enrique Lores' restructuring push to "recommit to the fundamentals" and become a technology company again, with more cuts expected over the next few years. The risk is losing the front-row seat to fintech innovation that the fund provided, leaving PayPal blind to emerging startups just as rivals keep their venture arms running.

title: NeuralTrust, which offers tech that lets enterprises discover, monitor, govern, and secure AI agents, raised a $20M seed led by Alstin Capital (Tamara Djurickovic/Tech.eu)
blurb: NeuralTrust raised a $20M seed led by Alstin Capital to help enterprises discover, monitor, govern, and secure the AI agents now spreading through their stacks. The round signals where the money is moving: not toward building more agents, but toward controlling the ones already deployed, where an unmonitored agent with system access is a liability nobody has fully priced. As companies push agents into production faster than their security teams can track them, the governance layer is becoming its own category, and the early movers are getting funded.

title: Blackstone’s AirTrunk Seeks $3 Billion Loan for Sydney Project
blurb: Blackstone-backed AirTrunk is in talks with banks for a A$4.3 billion ($3 billion) loan to fund a new Sydney data center, extending its debt-fueled buildout across Asia Pacific. The scale of the borrowing shows how much of the AI infrastructure race is now financed on leverage rather than equity, with operators racing to lock in capacity before demand catches up. For founders, it signals that compute capex is consolidating into a few heavily capitalized landlords, and the cost of that debt will eventually flow through to the price of inference.

title: Two Stanford grads raise $11M to build a noninvasive wearable for hormone tracking
blurb: Clair Health raised $11.6M led by Khosla Ventures to build a noninvasive wearable that tracks hormones rather than the steps and heart rate today's smartwatches measure. The pitch is that Apple Watch and Pixel sensors miss hormonal health entirely, so the company built its own sensor stack plus an AI voice layer that classifies a user's cycle phase after a few minutes of conversation. The bet is that women's health data, captured continuously across all four cycle phases and shareable with providers, becomes a wedge into perimenopause and menopause care that generic wearables can't reach.

title: China AI Lab’s 170% Stock Surge Cements Winner-Loser Pair Trade
blurb: Chinese AI stocks are now trading as a pair, with investors crowding into the perceived race winner after a 170% surge while shorting the rival they see falling behind. The market has stopped treating China's AI field as one rising tide and started picking who actually ships commercially viable models. For founders, it is a reminder that capital is moving past the "everyone wins" phase of AI and toward separating the labs with a real product path from the ones still burning compute to catch up.

title: Apple Reveals Plans to Raise Prices
blurb: Apple revealed plans to raise prices on its next iPhone lineup, breaking from years of holding the entry tier flat. The move lands as tariffs and higher component costs squeeze hardware margins across the industry, and Apple is choosing to pass that cost to buyers rather than absorb it. For founders selling physical or premium-priced products, it signals that the market leader thinks pricing power still holds, and that customers will tolerate increases when the brand and switching costs are strong enough.

title: Can Musk Pull Off a SpaceX-Tesla Merger?
blurb: Bloomberg Opinion columnist Liam Denning makes the case for folding SpaceX and Tesla into a single company, then lands on why it would be hard to do cleanly. The deal would pull a private rocket business and a public automaker under one roof, and the conflicts of interest run deep enough that minority Tesla shareholders would have a real claim to object. The harder question for founders watching is governance: when one founder controls multiple entities that increasingly share capital, talent, and technology, the line between synergy and self-dealing gets thin.

title: BWX Agrees to License Nuclear Reactor Design After Activist Push
blurb: BWX Technologies agreed to license its small modular reactor design after activist investor Ananym Capital Management pushed the company to commercialize the technology rather than keep it in-house. The move signals that SMRs are crossing from defense and R&D programs into licensable commercial assets, with an outside shareholder forcing the timeline. For founders building in energy or compute-heavy sectors, it marks nuclear shifting from a long-horizon bet to a near-term supply question, as activist pressure starts treating reactor IP like any other underused asset on the balance sheet.

title: Trump Says Anthropic Negotiations Continue as AI Leaders Huddle at G-7
blurb: Trump told reporters at the G-7 summit that talks with Anthropic are "going fine," even as the model shutdown that triggered them drags into its second week. The friction matters because some world leaders showed up worried about losing access to frontier AI tools their own governments and companies now depend on. For founders, the signal is that access to leading models has become a geopolitical variable, not a settled utility, and a single negotiation can throttle the infrastructure your product sits on.

title: Allbirds continues AI pivot with name change and CEO hire, sending stock soaring
blurb: Allbirds is changing its name to Smartbird and naming Nadia Carlsten, formerly head of AWS's quantum computing center and CEO of Nvidia-partnered DCAI, as its new chief executive, two months after pivoting from shoes to AI infrastructure. Shares jumped 39% on the news, extending a run that sevenfolded the market cap after April's rebrand. The pattern echoes the crypto-mania pivots of the last cycle, and a few of those worked: CoreWeave started as a crypto miner. The market is rewarding the AI label fast, before any compute revenue exists to back it.

title: Atom Computing raises $300M to build world’s first fault-tolerant, commercially viable quantum computer
blurb: Atom Computing raised $300M to accelerate its push toward a commercially viable, fault-tolerant quantum computer, with $100M from a Third Point Ventures-led Series C and another $100M committed by the U.S. Department of Commerce. The company is betting on neutral-atom qubits rather than the superconducting approach Google and IBM are chasing, and it recently became the second firm to publicly demonstrate quantum error correction after crossing 1,000 qubits. Government money sitting alongside venture capital signals that fault-tolerant quantum has moved from a research bet to a strategic one worth subsidizing.

title: Convey closes on $38M round to help companies automate repetitive work with AI teammates
blurb: Convey raised $38M in a Series A led by Andreessen Horowitz, with Khosla Ventures and Pear VC, to let nontechnical workers build and run autonomous AI teammates with no code. The pitch is a bet against the copilot model, which lifts productivity at the margins but leaves the mundane back-office work (pulling spreadsheet data, processing invoices, reconciling financials) on the employee. With global AI spend projected past $2.5 trillion this year and most enterprises still seeing little back-office payoff, the wager is that agents that own a task outright, deployable in about three hours, are what actually moves the number.

title: Pramaana Labs raises $27M to make AI prove its answers
blurb: Pramaana Labs raised $27M in seed funding for a system that checks an AI model's answer against a domain's formal rules and refuses to return it unless a proof engine can verify it's correct. The product targets regulated work where mistakes are expensive, starting with tax, medical diagnosis, cybersecurity, and financial compliance, and the company says it has yet to produce a confidently wrong verified answer. The bet is that LLMs can write an answer that sounds right but can't show it's right, and in rule-bound fields that proof is the part that matters.

title: Dear SaaStr:  My Top Sales Rep Made $600,000 a Year — And Just Quit!  What Happened?
blurb: Your top sales rep usually quits because of change, not money. A new VP of Sales arrives, the old one who sheltered them leaves, or next year's number simply looks harder to hit, and they bolt even when it means starting over for less. The rep thrived in the current environment and rarely reads change as good for them. You can't fully control it, but you can align any incoming VP with your top AE and pull that rep into the hiring process before the new boss lands.

title: Why we're bullish on loops
blurb: PostHog argues you should stop prompting agents and instead build self-prompting loops with a goal, context, evaluation, and an agent.

title: AVP et Earlybird lancent fonds de 500 millions d'euros dédié à la défense
blurb: AVP and Earlybird are launching a €500M paneuropean defense fund, a deliberate break from the domestic €100M to €150M vehicles that dominate the European market. The French-German pairing was built to give portfolio companies access to Germany's military budget as Berlin moves to make up for decades of underinvestment, with AVP bringing software and AI and Earlybird bringing historical deep tech. The fund targets around 20 companies at roughly €25M per ticket in Series B and beyond, with a first close expected by the end of June.

title: AI operating system startup Conduct closes £45m round
blurb: Conduct raised $60M in a Series A co-led by Index Ventures and ICONIQ, with strategic backing from SAP, to build an "AI operating system" for the bloated, heavily customized software that runs large enterprises. Decades of changes have left those systems opaque even to the people operating them, and that same opacity stops AI agents cold, because an agent can only act on a system it can understand. Conduct maps how every piece of custom code, config, and integration connects to the business logic it serves, betting that the unglamorous work of making legacy IT legible is where enterprise AI actually pays off.

title: Roelof Botha joins SpaceX’s board of directors
blurb: Roelof Botha joined SpaceX's board less than a week after the company's record-setting IPO, taking a seat on the audit committee alongside longtime Musk confidants. The former Sequoia managing partner brings public-company governance experience, but the board he joins has little real authority: Musk controls more than 80% of voting power and every change to the board's makeup. The signal for founders is that a marquee director hire doesn't always mean independent oversight, and in a founder-controlled company the board is there to advise, not to check the CEO.

title: Monument scores £18m seed for cloud banking platform
blurb: Monument Technology raised £18m in seed funding for its cloud Banking-Platform-as-a-Service, beating its initial £10m target. Founded in 2023, the UK firm stitches core banking, payments, and customer channels into one managed solution by integrating its own technology with existing components like Mambu, Salesforce, and Nice Actimize. The company has already opened a Series A at a higher valuation, a sign that demand for outsourced banking infrastructure is pulling capital forward even as financial institutions stay cautious about building these stacks in-house.

title: Why ICONIQ led a $60M Series A for a London startup fixing SAP
blurb: Conduct, a 35-person London startup spun out of Palantir, raised a $60M Series A co-led by Index Ventures and ICONIQ, with SAP investing strategically. The bet is timing: mainstream support for SAP ECC ends December 31, 2027, and roughly 17,000 of 35,000 ECC customers still haven't migrated. Conduct maps the custom code, settings, and dependencies buried in core SAP systems back to their business purpose, so teams can ask what a change will touch before they make it. Customers like Daimler Truck, DHL, and Fraport report transformation projects moving at least 30% faster.

title: Als eine US-Drohne versagt, überrascht ein Startup aus München im Kampf gegen russische Störsender
blurb: At a NATO electronic-warfare exercise in Lithuania, US units flooded the spectrum with jamming, yet Munich AI defense startup Helsing's HX-2 attack drone hit its target in 15 of 17 flights. When a US recon drone failed under the same jamming, American soldiers improvised and used the HX-2 to find targets too. That inverts a market that has run one way for decades, with Europe importing nearly all its flying hardware from the US. European defense startups now iterate against real Russian jamming alongside Ukrainian forces, and those combat-driven cycles are starting to beat US incumbents.

title: OpenAI kauft das Kieler KI-Startup Ona
blurb: OpenAI acquired Ona, the Kiel-based startup behind secure, reproducible cloud development environments, in what insiders call one of the largest German exits in years. The deal plugs a gap in Codex, OpenAI's coding assistant, which now has more than five million weekly users and grew 400% since January but needs a trusted place for agents to run multi-hour workflows inside a company's own infrastructure. For European founders, the read is that the frontier labs are now buying the operational layer that makes agents usable, and they are buying it from Europe.

title: At 21, Jenny Duan saw what Oura and WHOOP missed: women’s hormones. She raised $11.6M from Khosla Ventures to track them
blurb: Clair Health raised $11.6M led by Khosla to build the first noninvasive continuous hormone monitor for women, shipping November 2026.

title: Flagright secures $12.5M Series A to scale AI compliance platform
blurb: Flagright raised a $12.5M Series A led by Infinity Ventures to scale its AI operating system for financial crime compliance. The pitch is consolidation: one platform for transaction monitoring, watchlist screening, risk scoring, case management, and investigations, replacing the legacy systems and point solutions most institutions still stitch together. The differentiator is explainable AI, because regulated banks can only deploy models they can audit and defend, and the round goes toward expanding those use cases and pushing into the US market.

title: Next-gen nuclear company TerraPower plants flag in UK
blurb: TerraPower opened a UK subsidiary and started Step 1 of the country's Generic Design Assessment, its first move toward an international plant for its sodium-cooled Natrium reactor. The push tracks a broader nuclear resurgence driven by data center demand, electrification, and energy security, with the company already holding a Meta deal for up to eight reactors in the US. Next-gen nuclear is becoming a cross-border land grab, and the developers planting regulatory flags early are the ones positioned to supply the power-hungry compute buildout that everyone else is still scrambling to source.

title: Europe Bets Industrial AI Can Salvage Its Manufacturing Edge
blurb: Europe is racing to put AI on the factory floor, with Mistral, Siemens, and Schneider Electric leading a push to make industrial production more efficient. The bet is that Europe's deep manufacturing base, rather than consumer chatbots, is where it can still out-compete the US and China. The open question is whether shop-floor AI can move fast enough to offset high energy costs and slow industrial output before the lead erodes.

title: Undo raises £27.6m to boost software issue diagnosis by AI agents
blurb: Cambridge-based Undo raised $37m (£27.6m) led by Elsewhere Partners to give AI coding agents the runtime context they need to diagnose complex bugs. Its deterministic recording technology captures complete execution history, so agents can see what a program actually did rather than just what the code says, sharpening root-cause analysis across dev, test, and production. The bet is that as AI writes more code, the bottleneck shifts to debugging it, and the tooling that makes AI-generated systems understandable becomes infrastructure rather than a nice-to-have.

title: Israeli Startup M&A in H1 2026: Strategic Buyers Are Back
blurb: Israeli startup M&A kept pace in H1 2026 despite 2025's record $84B in exits, with Apple, Nvidia, Cisco, Palo Alto Networks, CrowdStrike, and ServiceNow all acquiring Israeli companies to fill strategic product gaps rather than chase growth stories. The new wrinkle is that Israeli and Israeli-rooted firms are increasingly buying other Israeli startups, a "blue-and-white" pattern that signals a maturing ecosystem. AI is the through-line in most deals, functioning less as a sector than as the acquisition rationale, with buyers paying for capabilities they believe they need to stay competitive.

title: Australia is the world’s fastest-growing venture ecosystem of the past decade
blurb: Australia is now the world's fastest-growing venture ecosystem, growing 13.7 times faster than any other major hub over the past decade and producing more $10B companies per dollar invested than anywhere else. The catch is that early-stage investment has fallen every year since 2021, and founders now raise 41% of their early-stage capital from overseas, double the share in Europe or the US. The headline returns are real, with Australian VC delivering a 24.4% pooled five-year return, but the domestic capital that should be funding the next Canva is barely reaching seed.

title: A robot is sprinting towards you. Do you want it running on Claude or Grok?
blurb: A developer at OpenRouter dropped eleven LLMs into a 2D battle royale and ran 30 games, finding that Grok 4.1 Fast won 13 matches at $0.97 per win while Claude Sonnet 4.6, which kept trying to form alliances, took second with 5 wins at $26.78 each. The post's takeaway is that standard benchmarks like Artificial Analysis failed to predict winners, and the model with the most kills, GPT 5.4, never topped the leaderboard.

title: Context engineering is the new analytics engineering skill: a practical guide for dbt users
blurb: The dbt blog argues that context engineering, the practice of structuring information so AI systems reason about a domain without hallucinating, is the same work analytics engineers already do when they write MetricFlow metric definitions, model descriptions, and schema contracts. The guide contrasts agents querying raw tables, which guess and are frequently wrong, against agents querying a governed dbt Semantic Layer, which return consistent, defensible answers.

title: Wolfram Language and Mathematica Version 15, AI Assistant, Symbolic Music, More
blurb: Wolfram released Version 15 of Wolfram Language and Mathematica, adding built-in AI assistant access, symbolic music representation, and a large batch of new core functionality across the language. The release also targets AIs as first-class users alongside humans, exposing interfaces designed to let LLM systems call Wolfram's computational engine for precise, formalized results rather than natural-language guesses.

title: Why is Meta destroying its engineering organization?
blurb: Gergely Orosz argues that Meta has dismantled its two-decade high-performance engineering culture in just a few weeks, recasting software engineering from a profit center into a disdained cost center through AI-mandated workflows and pressure on core engineers. He ties the shift to leadership treating infra teams as expendable, citing a recent high-profile outage and internal disarray as early evidence the demolition is already backfiring.

title: How we run Firecracker VMs inside EC2 and start browsers in less than 1s
blurb: Browser Use rebuilt its cloud browser infrastructure on Firecracker microVMs running inside EC2, giving every session its own isolated VM that starts in under a second. The nested-VM setup cut costs from $0.06 to $0.02 per browser hour and replaced a Unikraft unikernel design that handled idle shutdown well but couldn't autoscale fast enough during traffic spikes.

title: Stanford's DeLM cuts multi-agent task costs 50% — without a central orchestrator
blurb: Stanford researchers released DeLM, a decentralized multi-agent framework that drops the central orchestrator in favor of a shared knowledge base where agents coordinate directly off one another's verified progress. The approach cuts task costs by roughly 50% by removing the merge-filter-rebroadcast bottleneck, letting agents build on prior findings and recover detailed evidence only when needed.

title: Bringing more agent harnesses and frameworks to Cloudflare, starting with Flue
blurb: Cloudflare added durable execution, dynamic code execution, a durable filesystem, and dynamic workflows to its Agents SDK, exposing those primitives as a base layer for any agent harness or framework. Flue, a new open-source framework from the team behind Astro, is the first to build on it, shipping its 1.0 Beta this week atop the Pi harness.

title: All Smoke, No Alarm: Oracle Signals in Agent-Authored Test Code
blurb: A study of 86,156 test-file patches from 33,596 agent-authored PRs across five coding agents found that 80.2% contain weak or no explicit oracle signals, meaning the tests run code without actually verifying behavior. After adjusting for agent, PR size, and language, strong-oracle PRs were 28% more likely to merge, suggesting test-file counts overstate verification strength and quality gates should check for assertions, not just test presence.

title: Building AI Agents for AR Glasses and XR Devices with NVIDIA XR AI
blurb: NVIDIA released XR AI in public beta, an open source library for building AI agents that run inside AR glasses and XR headsets by routing live camera, microphone, and data streams to GPU-accelerated models in the cloud, data center, or edge. The agents combine visual grounding via NVIDIA Cosmos, voice interaction with Nemotron models, and enterprise tool calls over MCP, with early use in field service, manufacturing, and lab research.

title: Humiliating IIS servers for fun and jail time
blurb: This bug bounty walkthrough details a methodology for finding and exploiting IIS web servers, covering discovery via Shodan and Google dorks, fingerprinting through response headers like `Server: Microsoft-IIS/10.0`, and exploitation of common misconfigurations. The author also demonstrates bypassing WAFs using HTTP parameter pollution, treating the default IIS splash page as a signal of likely misconfiguration rather than a dead end.

title: Wear OS 7 helps your smartwatch keep up with you
blurb: Wear OS 7 is rolling out to eligible Pixel Watch devices, adding Android Live Updates on the wrist, cross-device media and earbud controls, and system-level power optimizations that deliver up to 10% better battery life over Wear OS 6. Select devices get Gemini Intelligence later this year, including natural-language widget creation via Create My Widget and multi-step app automation that runs tasks directly from the watch.

title: Homebrew 6.0 released with new security mechanism, Linux sandbox and more
blurb: Homebrew 6.0 shipped with a new security mechanism, a Linux sandbox for isolating formula builds, and other changes to the package manager's install pipeline. The sandbox confines build and installation steps on Linux to limit what untrusted formulae can touch, bringing Homebrew's Linux behavior closer to the tighter execution model it already applies on macOS.

title: [AINews] GLM-5.2: the top Frontend Coding model in the world, IndexShare for Speculative Decoding
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an MIT-licensed open-weight model with a 1M-token context window and two reasoning-effort modes, that independent leaderboards rank just behind Opus 4.8 on coding and ahead of every Opus on frontend coding. The 744B-parameter model ships with day-zero support across Transformers, vLLM, and SGLang, and uses a tweaked DeepSeek Sparse Attention variant to hold efficiency at ultra-long contexts.

title: Correlated randomness in Slay the Spire 2
blurb: The author shows that Slay the Spire 2's separate pseudorandom number generators are correlated, so observing one early RNG output leaks information that predicts the others, producing oddities like a 54% chance that Neow's Bones yields the Debt curse. The root cause is initializing each generator from the same seed plus a per-purpose hash, which leaves their first outputs statistically linked rather than independent.

title: How to Optimize Transformer-Based Models for Low-Precision Training
blurb: This NVIDIA guide shows how to translate a transformer config and batch size into the exact M×K×N GEMM shapes a model executes, then microbenchmark those shapes across BF16, FP8, and NVFP4 to predict where low precision actually pays off. Using a 5B-parameter CodonFM RNA model as the example, it leans on Transformer Engine for quantization and kernel dispatch so teams can estimate speedups on Hopper and Blackwell before committing to an expensive training run.

title: Getting more from each token: How Copilot improves context handling and model routing
blurb: GitHub Copilot in VS Code now cuts per-turn overhead with prompt caching, which reuses model state for repeated prompt prefixes, and tool search, which loads tool schemas on demand instead of sending every definition every turn. The goal is spending less of each session on recurring context and more on the task, with Auto routing also expanding so Copilot picks the model that fits the work without the developer choosing each time.

title: Decoupling Search from Reasoning: A Vendor-Agnostic Grounding Architecture for LLM Agents
blurb: This paper introduces Decoupled Search Grounding (DSG), which moves retrieval out of the reasoning model into an MCP-compatible gateway that exposes provider routing, fallback, retrieval-depth control, and caching as configurable controls. Across five frontier models, DSG nearly matches native search accuracy on SimpleQA (86.1% vs 87.7%) at 91% lower search cost, hits a 99.4% warm-cache rate with 68% lower latency, and cuts cost over 98% on an e-commerce workload.

title: Proactively reduce tech debt autonomously with AWS Transform – continuous modernization (preview)
blurb: AWS Transform – continuous modernization, now in preview, autonomously scans codebases across thousands of repositories against configurable baselines, then prioritizes tech debt findings and opens pull requests that remediate them. The capability targets end-of-life dependencies, deprecated frameworks, and outdated Lambda runtimes continuously rather than app-by-app, and lets platform teams codify their own policies, such as approved libraries or internal coding standards, to enforce across every repo.

title: AI demands more engineering discipline. Not less
blurb: The author argues that now that models like Opus 4.5 generate code on par with the median engineer, the bottleneck shifts from writing code to verifying it, making code review, tests, and immutable-infrastructure habits more important rather than less. They draw the analogy to the move from handcrafted server "pets" to immutable infrastructure, arguing teams that loosen discipline to move faster with AI are repeating a mistake that earned ops a decade of incidents.

title: The Value of Adaptivity in LSM Bloom-Filter Tuning: A Log-Law and a Two-Clock Frontier
blurb: This paper analyzes when adaptive Bloom-filter tuning in LSM trees actually beats the static Monkey optimum, deriving a log-law that makes optimal bits-per-key affine in the logarithm of access frequency and showing that coarse hotness estimates lose little because the workload enters only logarithmically. On Twitter production traces and a real RocksDB engine, reallocating filters only at compaction captures 96-99% of continuous tracking's benefit, since compaction already rebuilds filters for free on its own clock.

title: Agentic Resource Discovery: Let agents search
blurb: Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD) is an open specification, built by contributors from Microsoft, Google, GoDaddy, and Hugging Face, that lets agents search for and load tools, skills, and other agents at runtime instead of having URLs hardcoded into config files. It sits as a discovery layer in front of MCP, Skills, and A2A, defining a static `ai-catalog.json` manifest plus a `POST /search` registry, with Hugging Face's Discover Tool already indexing thousands of Spaces, Skills, and MCP servers.

title: Apple showcases its new developer AI tools in impressive 90-minute presentation
blurb: Apple published a 90-minute WWDC26 session, "Inside Apple Intelligence and Xcode," that demos the new Xcode 27, Foundation Models, Core AI, and MLX frameworks for developers. The standout segments build a full app with 3D animations and Visual Intelligence from a few prompts, and run the 1-trillion-parameter Kimi 2.6 model locally across four Mac Studios over RDMA-over-Thunderbolt.

title: SpaceX to acquire the AI coding startup Cursor for $60 billion
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire AI coding startup Cursor for $60B in stock, with the deal expected to close in Q3 pending regulatory approval. The purchase folds Cursor into SpaceX's xAI unit to compete with Anthropic and OpenAI on coding tools, though Cursor's market share has slipped from 41% to roughly 26% over the past year as Anthropic captured half the category.

title: Lore – Open source version control system designed for scalability
blurb: Epic Games released Lore, a centralized, content-addressed version control system that models repository state as Merkle trees over an immutable revision chain. It targets binary-heavy repos at scale through deduplication and sparse, on-demand hydration, positioning it against Perforce for game studios rather than Git's distributed model.

title: Apple's WebKit performance tax leaves iOS browsers stuck in the slow lane, says Microsoft
blurb: Microsoft argues that Apple's WebKit requirement forces all iOS browsers, including Edge, onto the same engine, leaving them unable to ship performance optimizations that competing engines deliver elsewhere. The complaint feeds into ongoing regulatory pressure under the EU's Digital Markets Act, which is meant to let third-party engines run on iOS but has yet to produce shipping alternatives.

title: ICYMI: OpenAI released CDP support for browser use on Codex
blurb: OpenAI added Chrome DevTools Protocol access to Codex, letting the agent profile JavaScript performance, read console output, network traffic, and rendered state, and rewrite a page's DOM directly from its embedded browser or Chrome. The opt-in developer mode is gated behind a Settings toggle, excluded from the EEA, UK, and Switzerland at launch, and still runs slowly and undertrained on the tooling, often needing several prompting attempts.

title: Firefox 152 understands “Sssh!”
blurb: Firefox 152 adds a Quick Action that silences every audio-playing tab across all windows when users type "mute," "sssh," or "hush" into the address bar. The release also ships experimental JPEG XL support, multi-tab sending and URL copying to other devices, a redesigned Settings layout, and roughly 40 security fixes.

title: PracRepair: LLM-Empowered Automated Program Repair Inspired by Human-Like Debugging Practices
blurb: PracRepair is an LLM-based automated program repair framework that builds on-demand static-dynamic context from failing executions, runs question-driven failure diagnosis to form explicit repair hypotheses, and refines patches using validation diagnostics and trace-level behavioral changes. On Defects4J V1.2/V2.0 it fixes 162/171 bugs under GPT-4o, beating state-of-the-art baselines and generalizing to real-world bugs across multiple models.

title: Start fresh, don't lift and shift: a dbt migration guide
blurb: This dbt migration guide argues that lift-and-shift migrations reproduce the old data model's problems in Jinja, leaving teams with one-to-one stored-procedure-to-model mappings, source-system names like salesforce_accounts_cleaned, no staging models, and hardcoded schemas instead of ref() lineage. It lays out six diagnostic symptoms of a legacy migration and argues teams should treat the move as a rewrite of the data model, not a mechanical port of existing code.

title: Git good with Epic Games' new open source VCS, Lore
blurb: Epic Games released Lore, an open source version control system built to handle the massive binary assets and monorepo scale that Git struggles with on large game projects. The system targets workflows where artists and engineers commit multi-gigabyte files, positioning it as an alternative to Perforce for studios that have outgrown Git's large-file handling.

title: Epic Games just took a big step toward AI-built games with Unreal Engine 5.8
blurb: Epic Games released Unreal Engine 5.8, the final UE5 release, with an experimental Model Context Protocol plugin that connects any LLM to core engine systems including blueprints, assets, levels, materials, and meshes. Epic demoed Claude Code pulling objects from an asset library and matching lighting to reference images, and framed the plugin as groundwork for UE6, where LLM integration is slated to become central to the creation pipeline.

title: ICYMI: ZAI launches GLM-5.2 open model with 1M context
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an open-weight coding model with a 1M-token context window and up to 128K output tokens, built for long-horizon agent work across full repositories. The 744B-parameter model scores 81.0 on Terminal-Bench 2.1 and 62.1 on SWE-bench Pro, uses an IndexShare sparse-attention scheme to cut per-token FLOPs 2.9x at 1M context, and plugs into Claude Code, Cline, and OpenClaw via an OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

title: Exploring Statistical Change Point Detection Techniques for Performance Anomaly Detection at Mozilla
blurb: Mozilla evaluated 25 change-point detection methods and 15 ensemble approaches against Perfherder's Student's T-test, which produces 12.5% false-positive alert groups and misses regressions in 6.8% of cases. Offline and hybrid CPD methods raised recall at a steep precision cost, but ensemble voting balanced the trade-off for an 11% F1 improvement, validated on a ground-truth dataset of 174 series annotated by eleven performance engineers.

title: Group Commit Self-Clocks: Why Tuning Is Unnecessary Above a Device-Set Load Threshold
blurb: This paper shows that group commit's classic tuning knob (timer, batch size, or adaptive policy) is vacuous in closed-loop OLTP, where each client issues its next transaction only after the last commits. Modeling commits as a closed queueing network, the authors prove that a parameter-free greedy policy self-clocks to within about 0.1% of the best oracle-tuned timer above a device-set load threshold, and confirm on PostgreSQL that commit_delay=0 stays competitive.

title: SteamOS Linux 3.8 released as stable
blurb: Valve promoted SteamOS 3.8 to its stable channel, shipping the Arch-based handheld OS with an updated Linux kernel, a newer Mesa graphics stack, and broader hardware support beyond the Steam Deck. The release continues Valve's push to run SteamOS on third-party handhelds and desktops, giving Linux gaming developers a more current Proton and driver baseline to target.

title: Clojure Hosted on Go
blurb: Glojure is an interpreter for Clojure hosted on Go, giving Clojure code direct access to Go libraries the way the JVM version reaches Java. As a hosted language, all Go values work as Glojure values and vice versa, though the project is early (v0.3.0), runs only a subset of core Clojure, and makes no backwards-compatibility guarantees before v1.

title: Position: Coding Benchmarks Are Misaligned with Agentic Software Engineering
blurb: The authors argue that coding benchmarks built in the pre-agent era are misaligned with how agents actually work, since they collapse model, harness, environment, and feedback into one end-to-end score graded against a single reference solution. They identify three failures: scores conflate the model with the surrounding harness, valid alternative solutions get penalized, and the lack of component-level signal makes the system hard to iterate on.

title: Want your images back? Sure... That'll be $5!
blurb: Photobucket now locks the images users uploaded for free behind a $5-per-month subscription, framing the paywall as "protecting" content the original owner can no longer access without paying. The author argues this is a deliberate dark pattern that bets on nostalgia and forgotten recurring charges, and treats it as a cautionary case for trusting any free host with data you care about keeping.

title: The Siren Song of  ariaNotify()
blurb: The new ariaNotify() method, defined in the WAI-ARIA 1.3 spec, lets developers programmatically trigger screen reader narration with a single string argument, replacing the brittle live-region workarounds that previously handled asynchronous content changes. The author walks through why live regions are so error-prone, then argues the new API should be reserved for cases that genuinely cannot be solved any other way, including pushing back on the feature itself.

title: Local Qwen isn't a worse Opus, it's a different tool
blurb: The author argues that local Qwen models aren't a budget Opus substitute but a distinct tool with a different reliability profile, drawing on production use across a bootstrapped software business rather than benchmark claims or airplane-coding anecdotes. An RTX 6000 Pro paid for itself within two or three months, but the post is candid about Qwen's worst failure modes: infinite loops and hallucination that worsen once you quantize down to fit a consumer GPU.

title: Linux kernel 7.1 sends Intel 486 support to silicon heaven
blurb: The Linux kernel 7.1 release drops support for Intel's 80486 and early 586 processors, removing decades-old workarounds for CPUs that lack the CMPXCHG8B and TSC instructions modern x86 code assumes. The cleanup deletes legacy emulation paths from the kernel's boot and locking code, simplifying maintenance for the 32-bit x86 architecture that remains in embedded and retro-computing use.

title: When the Next Step Is Not One Step: Distribution-Aware Execution Modeling for Concurrent Go Programs
blurb: This paper treats scheduler nondeterminism in concurrent Go programs as a training signal, running each program many times to build an empirical distribution of next events and fine-tuning a 7B model to match it with a KL objective. On 798 held-out predictions from real CockroachDB, Kubernetes, gRPC, and etcd bugs, it hits 36.2% accuracy and cuts Expected Calibration Error from 0.205 to 0.169 versus cross-entropy.

title: A Technical Taxonomy of LLM Agent Communication Protocols
blurb: This paper builds a technical taxonomy of LLM agent communication protocols, classifying nine actively maintained open-source protocols across five dimensions: counterparty, payload, interaction state, discovery mechanism, and schema flexibility. The authors find that all sampled agent-to-agent protocols pair hybrid payloads with session-state persistence and that decentralized discovery is rare, and they argue the field will converge toward a federated, layered protocol stack rather than a single winner.

title: Google Chrome update will close the door on ad blockers
blurb: Google is removing the final Manifest V2 workaround in Chrome 150, expected June 30, fully ending support for MV2 extensions including ad blockers like uBlock Origin. The change deletes the `ExtensionManifestV2Disabled` flag as dead code, with remaining MV2 flags slated for Chrome 151 in July, and Chromium-based browsers like Edge and Opera expected to follow.

title: HANSEL: Extracting Breadcrumbs from Web Agent Trajectories for Interactive Verification
blurb: HANSEL extracts interactive, verifiable evidence from AI web-agent trajectories, presenting navigable snapshots that preserve page state like applied filters and scroll position so users can trace how the agent reached its answer, and flagging answers that map to no visited page. On 45 AssistantBench and Online-Mind2Web tasks it hit 83.7% precision and 88.8% recall on evidence pages while cutting trajectory volume by 61.6%.

title: Launch HN: Adam (YC W25) – Open-Source AI CAD
blurb: Adam, a YC W25 startup, open-sourced CADAM, a browser-based tool that turns natural-language prompts and images into parametric OpenSCAD models exportable as .STL, .SCAD, or .DXF. It runs entirely client-side via WebAssembly, ships with the BOSL, BOSL2, and MCAD libraries, and includes interactive sliders for adjusting dimensions plus a benchmark suite covering full multi-part assemblies down to single parametric parts.

title: Smelly config files will make your agents waste tokens, researchers warn
blurb: Researchers found that poorly structured configuration files force coding agents to burn extra tokens re-parsing ambiguous or redundant context, with "smelly" configs measurably inflating the per-task cost of agent runs. The takeaway for engineers is to treat agent config like code, pruning dead settings and tightening structure so the model spends its context window on the task instead of decoding your setup.

title: Vercel launches a new framework and enterprise controls for agentic AI infrastructure
blurb: Vercel introduced a set of agentic AI infrastructure products at its Ship conference, led by Vercel Services, a deployment engine that ships front ends, back ends, and services from one commit to a single URL over private networks. The launch also includes eve, an open-source agent framework, the Agent Stack tooling bundle, and Vercel Connect, which swaps long-lived credentials for scoped short-lived tokens with full audit trails.

title: Agentic coding deserves more than a chat box bolted onto VS Code
blurb: Polypore is an open-source agentic desktop IDE built on Tauri 2, where every surface is a dockable sandboxed panel and the agent is the primary actor across the layout, memory system, debug tooling, and a bundled Node MCP sidecar exposing 22+ tools. Secrets stay in the OS keyring and are swapped for handle sentinels before any agent spawns, so the model drives panels and injects credentials over RPC without ever seeing plaintext.

title: Finding Compiler-Platform Interaction Bugs in Deep Learning Pipelines via Cross-Layer Constraints
blurb: XCheck tests deep learning compilers like TVM and ONNX-MLIR by deriving full-stack constraints that guide model generation and characterize compilation behavior across passes and hardware, rather than only validating input types. The approach surfaced 2,034 bug-revealing cases of compiler-platform interaction bugs, including memory overflows, integer overflows, and silent unexpected compilations that coverage and pass/fail signals miss.

title: SpaceX purchases Cursor, a Claude Code and OpenAI Codex competitor [U]
blurb: SpaceX acquired Cursor in a $60 billion deal, a week after the company went public and roughly two months after an initial $10 billion partnership granting Cursor access to xAI's Colossus infrastructure. The acquisition pulls a leading agentic coding tool, and a direct competitor to Anthropic's Claude Code and OpenAI's Codex, into SpaceX's expanding AI portfolio alongside xAI.

title: Understanding the rationale behind a rule when trying to circumvent it
blurb: Microsoft's "Old New Thing" argues that following a rule's letter without grasping its intent reproduces the exact failure the rule exists to prevent. The example is a driver whose process/thread callback correctly queues slow work to a System Worker Thread, then blocks waiting for it to finish, hanging the system because the real constraint was "return fast," not "delegate the work.

title: A Composable CRDT Layer for Byzantine-Resilient Deterministic Reconstruction
blurb: This paper introduces a delta-state CRDT layer that achieves Byzantine tolerance by decoupling update propagation from state derivation, incorporating all accepted updates while deriving state from only a valid subset. Implemented in Melda for JSON documents, the model provably converges under equivocation, omission, and reordering, and lets authentication, authorization, and confidentiality be layered on without affecting consistency.

title: Where Did the Variability Go? From Vibe Coding to Product Lines by Regeneration
blurb: The authors analyzed 10 vibe-coded C/C++ projects and found near-zero in-artifact variability, since the LLM resolves every configuration decision at generation time rather than at compile or runtime. They propose Variability by Regeneration, where the LLM derives a separate dead-code-free binary for each variant from a declarative spec and a dispatcher routes requests, arguing that variability in AI-generated software belongs in the specification rather than the code.

title: I admire Fabrice Bellard. He is almost certainly a better overall programmer
blurb: John Carmack praised Fabrice Bellard, the prolific French engineer behind FFmpeg, QEMU, and QuickJS, as almost certainly a better overall programmer than himself.

title: The Value of Adaptivity in LSM Bloom-Filter Tuning: A Log-Law and a Two-Clock Frontier
blurb: This paper asks when adaptive Bloom-filter tuning in LSM trees actually earns its complexity, building on Monkey's static optimum across synthetic sweeps, Twitter cache traces, and a real RocksDB engine. The authors find optimal bits-per-key is affine in the log of access frequency, so coarse hotness estimates lose little, and reallocating filters only at compaction captures 96-99% of continuous tracking's benefit. The takeaway is a three-regime policy that tells teams when fine-grained tracking is worth the machinery and when it isn't.

title: AWS enters the context layer race with a graph that learns from agents, not manual curation
blurb: AWS announced a context intelligence stack for AI agents, led by AWS Context, a knowledge graph service that automatically maps relationships across existing data and refines itself based on which sources agents actually use. It publishes all metadata as Apache Iceberg to S3 Tables queryable via Athena, Redshift, or Spark, and ships alongside GA of S3 Annotations and a preview of skill assets in Glue Data Catalog.

title: Ghost Vectors: Soft-Deleted Embeddings Remain Reconstructible in HNSW Vector Databases
blurb: This paper shows that "deleting" a vector from an HNSW database usually just flags the record, leaving the raw embedding on disk and fully reconstructible from the index files. Using Vec2Text with no fine-tuning, the authors recovered 25.5% of person names from a Wikipedia set and 100% of patient age and gender from synthetic health data, raising real GDPR Article 17 and HIPAA exposure. Their fix, Epoch Key Rotation, encrypts vectors and discards the key on deletion, dropping recovery to 0%.

title: Introducing new Explores and Merge Queries in Looker
blurb: Google added a suite of Gemini-powered AI assistants to Looker Explore, including an Insight Assistant that builds queries from natural language via the Conversational Analytics API and an Expression Assistant that writes custom calculations without requiring Looker Expression syntax. The update also ships AI-generated Explore summaries, auto-generated Quick Starts to remove the empty-canvas cold start, and a modernized UI with a resizable field picker and column-level contextual menus.

title: Databricks unveils CustomerLake, its agentic CDP
blurb: Databricks announced CustomerLake, an agentic CDP that runs natively on its lakehouse and is governed by Unity Catalog, consolidating identity resolution, audience building, campaign automation, and activation into one platform. The pitch is collapsing the gap between insight and action so the same models that generate customer context drive activation directly, rather than piping data out to disconnected martech tools, with Databricks claiming personalized experiences delivered 1 billion times a day.

title: Evaluating Learned Spatial Indexes
blurb: This paper benchmarks learned spatial indexes for range queries, isolating how block size, data and query skew, selectivity, and disk-versus-memory storage shift the refinement-versus-scan trade-off that determines latency. The authors run every index on a shared storage backend and standardized query pipeline, then distill the results into a workload-based decision tree for picking an index. Validated on real OpenStreetMap point sets with synthetic queries, the tree showed minimal decision regret and near-optimal performance.

title: Group Commit Self-Clocks: Why Tuning Is Unnecessary Above a Device-Set Load Threshold
blurb: This paper argues that group commit's release-timer tuning is mostly unnecessary in real OLTP workloads. Because clients issue their next transaction only after the last commits, arrivals are closed-loop, so a parameter-free greedy policy that flushes the moment the log device is free self-clocks to a fixed point within ~0.1% of the best oracle-tuned timer. The square-root timer rule only matters below a device-set load threshold; on PostgreSQL, commit_delay=0 stays competitive with any tuned value.

title: Data Intelligence Agents: Interpreting, Modeling, and Querying Enterprise Data via Autonomous Coding Agents
blurb: This paper introduces Data Intelligence Agents (DIA), a production system of three agents (Data Interpreter, Schema Creator, Query Generator) that treats autonomous coding agents as the core abstraction, generating, executing, validating, and repairing real artifacts instead of emitting text. The Query Generator matches or beats the best published results on all seven SQL benchmarks across four task types and four dialects in fully autonomous mode. The authors argue execution-grounded agents with shared memory generalize across the data integration workflow, with adaptation confined to natural-language instructions.

title: Key takeaways from day two of the Databricks Data + AI Summit
blurb: Databricks used day two of its Data + AI Summit to frame the escalating cost of agentic AI as the enterprise's central problem, with CEO Ali Ghodsi warning that token and compute consumption is "going to get extremely expensive." The company positioned new releases around that thesis: Omnigent as a "harness of harnesses" for agent supervision, and Unity AI Gateway adding cost budgets, smart model routing, and contextual security policies for governance.

title: Databricks’ new agentic coworker Genie One brings AI automation to every part of the business
blurb: Databricks launched Genie One at its Data + AI Summit, an agentic coworker that extends the existing Genie suite from conversational analytics to executing tasks across sales, marketing, and finance workflows. The tool reasons over structured and unstructured data via Genie Ontology, a self-improving context layer that scans an org's documents, applications, and data to ground agents in business context rather than guessing to fill the gaps.

title: Introducing the Agentic CDP: A New Species of CDP for a New Era of Agents
blurb: Databricks introduced the "Agentic CDP," a customer data platform built for AI agents as first-class operators, with always-on 1:1 personalization, embedded governed customer context, and a "Golden Context" layer that extends the traditional Customer 360 record. The pitch targets agentic buying cycles that run in milliseconds, arguing that batch-oriented CDPs are too slow and too coarsely segmented to reach buyers whose purchasing agents filter out anything less than precisely relevant.

title: Collaborative Large and Small Language Models for Accurate and Scalable Data Repair
blurb: LasRepair pairs an LLM with a small language model for data repair, using the LLM as an instructor that selects a global repair context to guide a cheaper SLM corrector rather than repairing rows directly in their noisy raw context. Two extensions push accuracy further: an EM-style loop that alternates between updating the corrector and refining the context, and column-calibrated confidence weighting that down-weights uncertain rows. On real-world datasets, the full version beats the strongest baseline by 18.1% F1.

title: Wear OS 7 helps your smartwatch keep up with you
blurb: Google released Wear OS 7 to eligible Pixel Watch devices today, bringing Android Live Updates to the wrist for glanceable real-time tracking, cross-device media and earbud controls, and up to 10% better battery life over Wear OS 6. Select devices get Gemini Intelligence later this year, including Create My Widget for building custom dashboards through natural language and multi-step app automation that completes tasks directly from the watch.

title: Anthropic ships major Claude Design overhaul with design system imports, code round-trips, and a fix for its token-burning problem
blurb: Anthropic overhauled Claude Design, adding design-system imports from GitHub repos or design files, code round-trips, and a rebuilt editor that cuts the heavy token consumption that let early users exhaust a weekly Claude Pro allowance in under half an hour. The update repositions the tool from a prototyping demo into a brand-compliance layer that checks generated UI against imported components and auto-corrects before export.

title: Apple confirms price increases are coming to its products due to RAM shortage
blurb: Apple confirmed that price increases across its product line are coming, with CEO Tim Cook telling The Wall Street Journal the hikes are "unavoidable" as DRAM and storage costs surge and AI-server demand for high-bandwidth memory squeezes consumer supply. Macs and iPads could see increases before the September iPhone 18 launch, and the squeeze lands just as Apple's cheapest laptop, the 8GB MacBook Neo, leans on the same constrained memory.

title: Claude Design will now stick to your brand guidelines instead of generic AI mockups
blurb: Anthropic updated Claude Design, its prompt-to-prototype tool, so it now imports design systems from GitHub repos, design files, or uploaded assets and checks every output against those approved components before showing it. The release also adds a /design-sync handoff to Claude Code, admin-locked org-wide systems, a rebuilt canvas editor, and export paths to Adobe, Canva, Figma-adjacent tools, PDF, and PowerPoint.

title: iA Writer 8 brings Liquid Glass design, clear icon support, more
blurb: iA Writer 8 for iPhone, iPad, and Mac ships a Liquid Glass redesign that fits in on iOS 26 and macOS Tahoe, with app icons that mirror system appearance settings, including Apple's new clear style. The update also adds a Command Palette for formatting and export actions, a searchable Document Outline, a compact Library layout, and author-color cues in the Authorship view.

title: Apple releasing 20th anniversary iPhone, AirPods with cameras next year: report
blurb: Apple is preparing a late-2027 lineup headlined by a 20th-anniversary iPhone with a nearly edge-to-edge display and curved glass wrapping the sides, plus a second-generation foldable and camera-equipped AirPods, all built on a 2nm A21 chip, according to a Bloomberg report from Mark Gurman. The AirPods, which use visual AI models to identify objects in a user's surroundings, slipped from 2026 to 2027 amid Apple's ongoing Siri and Apple Intelligence delays.

title: VSCO launches Studio Pro mobile photo editing app and plans $500 per year subscription
blurb: VSCO launched Studio Pro, an iOS photo editor built for high-volume jobs like weddings and events, with batch editing, reference-image style matching, and Galleries delivery; a macOS version and RAW support arrive later this year. It anchors the $500-per-year VSCO One bundle, priced at parity with Adobe Creative Cloud Pro and pitched as a single pipeline from editing through client delivery.

title: NBBJ wraps Vivo skyscraper in spiralling terrace
blurb: NBBJ completed Vivo's 150-metre Shenzhen headquarters in the Qianhai Bay business district, wrapping the 6,000-person tower in a terrace that spirals around its exterior. The continuous outdoor band breaks from the sealed-glass curtain-wall norm for Chinese corporate towers, treating accessible exterior space as a primary facade element rather than an amenity tacked onto the roof.

title: Affinity Will Now Come Pre-Installed on Windows Surface Computers
blurb: Microsoft will pre-install Canva's Affinity design suite on its new Snapdragon X2 Surface laptops, pinning the free professional photo, design, and publishing app to the Start menu and wiring haptic feedback into the touchpad and Slim Pen for Affinity editing. The deal is a notable distribution win for Affinity, which added three million users in the month after relaunching as a free tool late last year.

title: Midjourney, the AI image generator, is developing a full-body ultrasonic scanner
blurb: Midjourney, best known for its text-to-image AI, announced Midjourney Medical and its first hardware product: a scanner that submerges you in water and uses a ring of half a million ultrasonic emitters to build a sub-millimeter 3D body map in under 60 seconds. Built with Butterfly Network under an exclusive ultrasound-on-chip license, the machine targets MRI-quality imaging at roughly 100x the speed, with company-run scanning spas planned over the next 12 months.

title: How Gaudí's Crypt paved the way for parametricism
blurb: Gaudí's hanging chain models for the Church of Colònia Güell, begun in 1898, used weighted strings and mirrors to derive structural form from gravity itself, computing catenary curves a century before software could. The technique prefigures parametric design's core idea: define the rules and constraints, then let the system generate the geometry rather than drawing it by hand.

title: The Siren Song of  ariaNotify()
blurb: The new ariaNotify() method, defined in the WAI-ARIA 1.3 spec, lets developers programmatically trigger screen reader narration with a single string argument, replacing the brittle workarounds teams previously built on top of ARIA live regions. The author makes the case for treating it as a last resort, reserved for problems that genuinely cannot be solved otherwise, including pushing back on the feature that created the need.

title: A2UI under the hood: Designing for the new era of radically adaptive UI
blurb: A2UI is an open protocol from Google, now shaped by CopilotKit and others, that lets an AI assemble interfaces on the fly from a designer-defined catalogue of components rather than generating raw div-and-Tailwind markup. The author argues this "radically adaptive UI" approach keeps designers in control of the building blocks while the screen reassembles itself around each request, though the spec is young and the design-to-code pipeline does not exist yet.

title: Epic Games details how it's embracing generative AI in Unreal Engine
blurb: Epic Games detailed plans to build generative AI tooling directly into upcoming versions of Unreal Engine, spanning asset creation and in-editor workflows for developers. The move pushes gen AI from third-party plugins into the engine's core pipeline, raising familiar questions about training-data provenance and how much of a game's art can be machine-generated before studios and players push back.

title: How&How gives Brunel's SS Great Britain a second life as Bristol Dockyards
blurb: How&How rebranded Brunel's SS Great Britain as the Bristol Dockyards, expanding the single ship into a three-part cultural destination spanning the Being Brunel museum, the Brunel Institute archive, and the vessel itself. The identity leans on a collage system that layers 200 years of timelines, typography, and dockyard ephemera, plus a defiantly un-nautical pink pulled from Totterdown's terraced houses to counter slipping ticket sales and an ageing audience.

title: VSCO unveils Studio Pro app as it pushes further into professional photography workflows
blurb: VSCO released Studio Pro on iOS, a pro-focused editor that batch edits up to 100 photos at once and includes Style Match, which analyzes any image and rebuilds its color, tone, and mood from a tailored mix of presets. The app slots into VSCO's expanding business stack alongside Workspace, Canvas, and Sites, and anchors the upcoming $499.99/year VSCO One bundle, with a macOS version due later this year.

title: Siri AI will make the Apple Watch a fully-fledged AI wearable in watchOS 27
blurb: Apple will bring its overhauled Siri AI and a dedicated Siri app to the Apple Watch in watchOS 27, extending the Apple Intelligence stack to the wrist for the first time after the device sat out the 2024 rollout. The pitch is an always-on, screen-free assistant available even without an unlocked iPhone or AirPods nearby, and watchOS 27 requires Series 9, Ultra 2, or SE 3 and later.

title: Epic Games says Unreal Engine 6 will help developers "build content faster" using AI models
blurb: Epic Games detailed Unreal Engine 6, which moves gameplay programming to Verse, makes content and economies portable across games via open standards, and bakes in generative AI through an MCP with Claude and Gemini integrations. Early access is targeted for late 2027 with a full release 12 to 18 months later, and the company is shipping Unreal Engine 5.8 as the current branch's final major update.

title: How Taylor Swift, Lego and Star Wars nail brand collaboration
blurb: Strategic fit, authenticity, and a genuine cultural moment separate standout brand collaborations like Lego x Star Wars from forced ones.

title: No Liquid Glass slider for Apple Watch, but here’s how watchOS 27 design changes
blurb: Apple's Liquid Glass opacity slider, which lets iPhone, iPad, and Mac users dial the material between clear and frosted in iOS 27, is not coming to Apple Watch, where watchOS 27 keeps a single fixed setting for everyone. Watch users still get the broader Liquid Glass refresh seen across the iPhone, just with subtler translucency tweaks and no control over how much frosting appears.

title: 2026 Core77 Design Awards Autodesk Fusion Prize - Otter Newborn Warmer by Design That Matters
blurb: Design That Matters won the 2026 Autodesk Fusion Prize for Otter, a portable conductive warmer treating newborn hypothermia in low-resource hospitals.

title: Storied Colors – A catalogue of named colors
blurb: Storied Colors is an online catalogue that documents one historical pigment a day, tracing each color's provenance, chemistry, and human cost, from where it was first ground to when it was banned and what replaced it. The site indexes 252 entries so far, adds new ones every Sunday at 06:00 GMT, and amends older entries as conservation labs publish corrections.

title: Microsoft just gave Outlook for Mac an ‘app-wide’ Liquid Glass update
blurb: Microsoft updated Outlook for Mac with an app-wide Liquid Glass refresh, refactoring the New button's motion, adding elevation and glass effects to profile controls, and widening corner radii to match Tahoe-native chrome. The release also adds PST import for migrating mail, calendars, and contacts, and targets parity with Apple's macOS 27 Golden Gate iteration of the design language.

title: Massive breach spills credentials for thousands of sensitive networks
blurb: Researchers disclosed a credential-spraying campaign against internet-facing FortiGate firewalls that compromised nearly 74,000 devices across 194 countries, dumping plaintext credentials for organizations including Oracle, Chevron, Lenovo, FedEx, a NATO defense contractor, and Fortinet itself. Roughly half of all exposed Fortinet firewalls are affected and most remain online with valid credentials, so operators should treat compromised devices as live, rotate credentials, and audit downstream RADIUS and Active Directory access immediately.

title: Microsoft Teams Relay Servers Abused in DragonForce Ransomware Attack
blurb: DragonForce ransomware operators deployed Backdoor.Turn, a Go-based implant that abuses Microsoft's TURN relay infrastructure to disguise C2 traffic as legitimate Microsoft Teams sessions, which Symantec calls the first malware family to weaponize TURN this way. The actor gained access to a US services firm via an unpatched SQL/MSSQL server in December 2025, used DLL sideloading and a BYOVD driver exploit for kernel-level access, then disabled security processes before encrypting and exfiltrating data.

title: GhostTree Attack Abused Recursive Windows Junctions to Hide Malware
blurb: Researchers disclosed GhostTree, a Windows evasion technique that abuses NTFS junctions pointed at their own parent directory to create recursive loops generating effectively infinite file paths, causing recursive scanners and some EDR products to hang before they ever examine malware in the folder. Junction creation requires only write access and no admin privileges, so defenders should constrain directory recursion depth and flag reparse points that loop back on their own ancestors.

title: Microsoft Confirms RoguePlanet Defender Zero-Day, Says Patch is in Development
blurb: Microsoft confirmed a Defender zero-day codenamed RoguePlanet (CVE-2026-50656, CVSS 7.8), a race condition in the Malware Protection Engine that hands attackers a SYSTEM-level shell regardless of whether real-time protection is enabled. No patch has shipped yet, public PoC code from researcher Chaotic Eclipse already exists, and this is the fourth Defender flaw the same researcher has disclosed after BlueHammer, UnDefend, and RedSun.

title: New Rokarolla Android Malware Steals PINs, SMS Codes, and Crypto Wallet Funds
blurb: Zimperium's zLabs documented Rokarolla, a new Android banking trojan that targets 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps with 137 remote commands, lifting lock-screen PINs via fake-lockscreen overlays, intercepting SMS one-time codes, rewriting the clipboard to redirect crypto payments, and disabling Google Play Protect. It spreads through malicious sites impersonating apps like TikTok and Chrome, installing a dropper disguised as Play Protect that abuses Accessibility access and rotates through multiple fallback C2 domains.

title: FortiBleed leak exposes Fortinet VPN credentials for 73,000 devices.
blurb: A leak dubbed "FortiBleed," discovered by researcher Bob Diachenko, exposed valid FortiGate SSL VPN credentials, including plaintext passwords, for 73,932 firewall URLs at organizations including Chevron, Samsung, AT&T, and Mercedes-Benz. Diachenko attributes the haul to a Russian-speaking group that intercepted and cracked SSL VPN authentication hashes; affected organizations should treat exposed appliances as compromised, force password resets, and audit Active Directory for lateral movement.

title: Chrome and Firefox Updated to Patch Critical, High-Severity Vulnerabilities
blurb: Google and Mozilla shipped updates patching over 70 vulnerabilities across Chrome and Firefox, including seven critical use-after-free flaws in Chrome 149 and 13 high-severity memory safety bugs in Firefox 152, several exploitable for RCE or sandbox escape. None are known to be exploited in the wild, but admins should push the Chrome 149.0.7827.155/.156 and Firefox 152 builds, plus the parallel ESR, Thunderbird, and iOS fixes, before public PoCs emerge.

title: Google Vertex AI SDK Flaw Let Attackers Hijack Model Uploads via Bucket Squatting
blurb: A flaw in Google Cloud's Vertex AI SDK for Python (fixed in 1.148.0) let an attacker who knew only a victim's public project ID pre-create the predictable default staging bucket, redirecting the victim's model upload to attacker-controlled storage and swapping in a malicious pickle that ran code inside Vertex AI's serving container. Palo Alto Unit 42, which reported the "Pickle in the Middle" bug, saw no in-the-wild exploitation; update the SDK and set an explicit staging_bucket.

title: Crooks found a new way to collaborate using Teams – by hiding command-and-control traffic
blurb: Attackers are abusing Microsoft Teams as a covert command-and-control channel, tunneling C2 traffic through legitimate Teams messaging to blend in with normal enterprise collaboration and evade network monitoring. Defenders should inspect Teams traffic for anomalous automation, restrict external federation, and alert on processes spawning Teams API calls outside expected user behavior.

title: PhantomSkill: Malicious Code Injection in Agent Skill Ecosystems
blurb: This paper presents PhantomSkill, an attack framework that hides malicious behavior in the auxiliary resources of third-party agent skills rather than their text descriptions, using a technique called VulMask to rewrite overt malware into trigger-activated, vulnerability-shaped code that evades automated reviewers. The authors argue skill ecosystems need resource-level vetting and execution-time containment, treating exploitable vulnerabilities in agent skills as potential malicious payloads.

title: Cisco adds another SD-WAN box to max-severity bug advisory
blurb: Cisco expanded its max-severity (CVSS 10.0) SD-WAN advisory to cover another affected appliance, broadening the set of devices vulnerable to the unauthenticated remote code execution flaw. Operators running Cisco SD-WAN gear should map their inventory against the updated advisory and apply the fixed releases, treating edge-exposed boxes as priority since the bug allows full takeover without credentials.

title: Cybercriminals allegedly hacked tens of thousands of Fortinet firewalls used by major companies all over the world
blurb: Two cybersecurity firms, Hudson Rock and SOCRadar, disclosed an ongoing campaign dubbed FortiBleed that has compromised more than 30,000 internet-exposed Fortinet firewalls and VPNs using credential bruteforcing and reused passwords from prior breaches, with named victims including Accenture, Comcast, Oracle, and Samsung. No new CVE is involved, so defenders should rotate all firewall and VPN credentials, kill reused passwords, and check exposed devices for unauthorized sessions and credential-harvesting activity.

title: AWS Security Agent adds threat modeling, Kiro power and Claude Code plugin, and more
blurb: AWS Security Agent, part of AWS Continuum, added STRIDE-based threat modeling and full-repository code review (both in preview), with pull request scanning, remediation commits, and integrations for GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and Confluence. A new MCP integration plus a Claude Code plugin and Kiro support let teams run code reviews, generate threat models, and apply fixes inline from any IDE or CLI.

title: Massive password-stealing attack hits 75k Fortinet firewalls
blurb: A credential-harvesting campaign has compromised roughly 75,000 internet-facing Fortinet firewalls, scraping administrator and VPN passwords from exposed appliances. Operators running affected FortiGate devices on the edge should assume captured credentials are already in adversary hands, rotate all admin and VPN secrets, and audit logs for unauthorized configuration changes and rogue accounts.

title: SprySOCKS Windows Variant Abuses Kernel Drivers to Evade Detection
blurb: ESET documented a previously undocumented Windows variant of SprySOCKS, a backdoor used by the China-nexus group FishMonger (aka Earth Lusca, Aquatic Panda), deployed against government targets in Honduras, Taiwan, Thailand, and Pakistan during 2023 and 2024. The WIN_DRV build chains two encrypted kernel drivers, DriverLoader (fsdiskbit.sys) and RawWNPF, to hide the backdoor's processes and files by intercepting system calls and to kill security tooling.

title: Junior Hacker Used Tailscale and OpenSSH to Keep Access After His C2 Went Offline
blurb: Cato Networks tracked a junior French-speaking operator nicknamed "Poisson" through a 33-day intrusion at a small French automotive firm, capturing 339 commands after he left SSH keys and a playbook in an open Backblaze B2 bucket. Before his Havoc C2 went dark, he installed OpenSSH and Tailscale on a victim machine for out-of-band persistence, so taking the C2 offline failed to remediate the compromise.

title: Detecting Hidden ML Training With Zero-Overhead Telemetry
blurb: This paper tests whether GPU compute-governance monitoring can survive an adversary trying to hide training runs. Using only zero-overhead, privacy-preserving NVML telemetry, the authors built a classifier that hits 98.2% binary accuracy at flagging training workloads across 9 GPU models and 4 architecture generations, and holds 43-87% accuracy against adversarially disguised workloads over 5 rounds of monitor-evader iteration. The takeaway for governance schemes is that content-agnostic side-channel signals are robust enough to detect hidden training without ever touching model weights or data.

title: CodeSentinel: A Three-Layer Defense Against Indirect Prompt Injection in Code Contexts
blurb: CodeSentinel is an inference-time sanitizer that defends code LLMs against indirect prompt injection hidden in retrieved context like comments, strings, identifiers, and decoy code. It uses Tree-sitter to extract high-risk CST nodes, then applies syntax-guided pre-filtering, CST-guided Dynamic Min-K% scoring, and node perturbation analysis to detect and neutralize adversarial triggers before they reach the model. Across six attack families it hit 0.80 average node-level F1, outperforming CodeGarrison, DePA, and KillBadCode.

title: Ghost Vectors: Soft-Deleted Embeddings Remain Reconstructible in HNSW Vector Databases
blurb: Researchers showed that "soft-deleted" embeddings in HNSW vector databases remain physically recoverable from raw index files, bypassing the API, and used the off-the-shelf Vec2Text inversion model to reconstruct PII from RAG corpora at the storage layer. Recovery hit 25.5% of exact person names and 46.4% of locations on Wikipedia data, 100% of patient age and gender on Synthea records, and 99% top-1 facial-identity matches, undermining GDPR Article 17 and HIPAA erasure claims.

title: OpenAnt: LLM-Powered Vulnerability Discovery Through Code Decomposition, Adversarial Verification, and Dynamic Testing
blurb: OpenAnt is an open-source pipeline that pairs static analysis with LLM reasoning to find vulnerabilities in large codebases, decomposing them into reachability-filtered units to cut the analysis surface by up to 97%. Candidate bugs pass through adversarial verification under simulated attacker constraints, then dynamic validation in auto-generated, sandboxed exploit environments. On OpenSSL, WordPress, and Flowise, the closed-loop approach surfaced previously unknown vulnerabilities while substantially reducing the false positives that make static scanners hard to triage.

title: Humiliating IIS servers for fun and jail time
blurb: A bug bounty researcher details a methodology for finding and exploiting Microsoft IIS servers, using Shodan and Google dorks to surface forgotten staging boxes, admin panels, and internet-facing internal tools, then probing for common misconfigurations and bypassing WAFs via HTTP parameter pollution.
